content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, the refactor agent, or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	- archon-lean-lsp: Lean LSP MCP server (project scope) — use for all Lean LSP operations (search, diagnostics, goal inspection)
19	- archon-informal-agent: `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` (symlinked from Archon) — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini) for informal mathematical reasoning
20	
21	## Key Files & Permissions
22	
23	All state files are in `.archon/`:
24	
25	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Refactor Agent | Review Agent | User |
26	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
27	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
28	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
29	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
30	| `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` | **write** | do not read | **read** (then execute) | do not read | read |
31	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
32	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | read only | read only | read |
33	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (refactor.md) | read only | read |
34	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
35	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
36	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read + limited write** (file path rename only) | read | **write** |
37	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | **write (all files; protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed)** | do not edit | write (via comments) |
38	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
39	
40	## Protected Declarations
41	
42	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
43	
44	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
45	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
46	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed. 
47	
48	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
49	
50	The blueprint uses two active markers.
51	
52	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
53	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
54	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
55	
56	**The review agent is the sole writer of these markers.** Prover agents (autoformalize / prover / polish) never touch the blueprint chapters — they describe their outcome in `task_results/<file>.md` and the review agent verifies and marks accordingly. The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints, but never markers.
57	
58	## User Interaction
59	
60	Users provide hints in two places:
61	
62	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
63	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
64	
65	## Agent Roles
66	
67	### Plan Agent
68	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
69	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
70	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
71	- Read `.archon/task_results/refactor.md` - if refactor agent has run, read their report and adjust your plans accordingly
72	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
73	- Write `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` when structural changes are needed
74	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
75	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
76	
77	### Prover Agent
78	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
79	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
80	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
81	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
82	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
83	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
84	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
85	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
86	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
87	
88	### Refactor Agent
89	- Read `.archon/prompts/refactor.md` for your full instructions
90	- Read `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` for the plan agent's directive
91	- Modify any `.lean` file: definitions, signatures, types, imports
92	- Keep all files compiling (insert `sorry` at broken proof sites)
93	- Do NOT fill proofs — that's the prover's job
94	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/refactor.md`
95	
96	### Review Agent
97	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
98	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
99	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
100	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
101	- **Update blueprint markers** (`\leanok`, `\mathlibok`) in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` based on verified compilation and the provers' task results
102	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md

3	## Current Stage
4	prover  (iteration 012 — Path-2 Čech scaffold sub-step, **prover round**. The iter-012 refactor agent has executed the directive: two new `:= sorry` declarations were appended to `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean` (`Scheme.cechCochain_OC` at L228 and `Scheme.cechCohomology_OC` at L241). Plan-agent post-refactor verification (this pass) confirms exactly the expected post-refactor state: sorry count `9 → 11`, two `declaration uses sorry` warnings on L228/L241 only, `Genus.lean` still kernel-only, `archon-protected.yaml` unchanged, both prover-round closure bodies re-probed `{success: true, diagnostics: []}` against the post-refactor file. The iter-012 prover round closes both new sorries with the probe-confirmed one-liners.)

7	- [x] init

12	## Big-picture context
13	
14	This project formalizes the Jacobian of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field, following Christian Merten's challenge (`references/challenge.lean`). The nine declarations in `archon-protected.yaml` are the deliverables — their signatures are frozen.
15	
16	`STRATEGY.md` describes the multi-phase build-out path (Phases A–E). Iter-005/006/007 closed Phase A steps 1–5 + polish. Iter-008 closed Track 2 (étale sheafification of `PicardFunctorAb`). Iter-009 closed the Phase A step 5/6 bridge (`HModule` infrastructure for `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf cohomology). Iter-010 closed the Phase A step 6 *algebraic preliminary* (`HModule_zero_linearEquiv`). Iter-011 closed the **first protected sorry** (`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`). Iter-012 opens Path-2 of Phase A step 6 (Čech cohomology of the structure sheaf, the multi-iteration route to Serre finiteness): the iter-012 refactor agent has scaffolded the Čech cochain complex and its `n`-th cohomology; the iter-012 prover round (this pass's objectives) closes both with the probe-confirmed one-liners.
17	
18	## Iteration 012 post-refactor verification (this pass)
19	
20	Plan-agent verification of the iter-012 refactor (executed under `task_results/refactor.md`, status `COMPLETE`):
21	
22	- **Sorry count**: `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/sorry_analyzer.py" AlgebraicJacobian/ --format=summary` returns `11 total across 4 file(s)`. Distribution: 5 `Jacobian.lean` + 3 `AbelJacobi.lean` + **2 `Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean`** (new) + 1 `Picard/Functor.lean`. Pre-refactor: 9; post-refactor: 11. Matches `9 + 2 = 11` exactly.
23	- **Compilation `Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean`**: `lean_diagnostic_messages` returns exactly two warnings — `declaration uses 'sorry'` at L228 col 19 and L241 col 19 — zero errors, zero failed dependencies. The two warning sites coincide with the new declaration openers; all earlier closures (iter-005 prereqs, iter-006 main, iter-007 polish, iter-009 `HModule`, iter-010 `HModule_zero_linearEquiv`) are intact.
24	- **Compilation `Genus.lean`**: `lean_diagnostic_messages` returns `{success: true, items: [], failed_dependencies: []}` (zero warnings, zero errors). The iter-011 honest closure of `AlgebraicGeometry.genus` is preserved.
25	- **`archon-protected.yaml`**: untouched.
26	- **Signatures match directive (modulo two refactor-agent name-resolution adjustments)**:
27	  - `Scheme.cechCochain_OC` at L228: `noncomputable def Scheme.cechCochain_OC {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) {ι : Type u} (𝒰 : ι → TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat) : CochainComplex (ModuleCat.{u} k) ℕ := sorry`
28	  - `Scheme.cechCohomology_OC` at L241: `noncomputable def Scheme.cechCohomology_OC {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k))) {ι : Type u} (𝒰 : ι → TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat) (n : ℕ) : ModuleCat.{u} k := sorry`
29	
30	  Two refactor-agent adjustments documented in `task_results/refactor.md`:
31	  1. The two declarations are placed inside `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` (not `namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme`) so the `def Scheme.cechCochain_OC` form yields the full name `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCochain_OC` (rather than the duplicated `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Scheme.cechCochain_OC` that arises if placed inside `namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme`).
32	  2. The parameter type `Opens C.left.toTopCat` is spelled `TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat` because writing `def Scheme.foo` auto-opens the `Scheme` sub-namespace for elaboration, and inside `Scheme` the bare `Opens` resolves to `Scheme.Opens` (which expects a `Scheme`, not `TopCat`). This convention matches the iter-006 declarations in this same file.
33	
34	  Both adjustments are name-resolution-only; the mathematical content is verbatim per the directive. The full declaration names — `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCochain_OC` and `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCohomology_OC` — match the directive exactly.

Both prover-round bodies are drop-in.

40	## Current Objectives
41	
42	Single-file objective. The iter-012 prover round closes two scaffold sorries in the same file (`Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean`); both are one-liner term-mode bodies that the plan-agent live probe (this pass) has confirmed against the post-refactor project state.
43	
44	### 1. **`AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean`** — close the two iter-012 Čech-scaffold sorries (L228, L241).
45	
46	**Blueprint reference:** `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`, the iter-012-added section "Čech cohomology of the structure sheaf" containing `def:Scheme_cechCochain_OC` and `def:Scheme_cechCohomology_OC`.
47	
48	#### Goal
49	
50	Close two `:= sorry` bodies with probe-confirmed term-mode one-liners. The two declarations live in `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` (lines 214–247 of the file), and have full names `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCochain_OC` / `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCohomology_OC`.
51	
52	#### Probe-confirmed closure bodies
53	
54	Replace each `sorry` body with the corresponding term-mode body below. **Both have been re-probed (`lean_run_code`) by the plan agent against the post-refactor file with `{success: true, diagnostics: []}`** — they are drop-in, no adjustment expected.
55	
56	```lean
57	-- L228: Scheme.cechCochain_OC body
58	(cechComplexFunctor 𝒰).obj ((sheafToPresheaf _ _).obj (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C))
59	```
60	
61	```lean
62	-- L241: Scheme.cechCohomology_OC body
63	(Scheme.cechCochain_OC C 𝒰).homology n
64	```
65	
66	Notes on name resolution (relevant if Lean elaboration surfaces a unification ambiguity that the live probe missed):
67	
68	- The file has `open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry` at L38. Inside `namespace AlgebraicGeometry` (the new declarations' namespace) `cechComplexFunctor` resolves directly to `CategoryTheory.cechComplexFunctor`, and `sheafToPresheaf` resolves to `CategoryTheory.sheafToPresheaf` — both visible without further prefixes. `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf` resolves to `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.toModuleKSheaf` (auto-prefixed under `namespace AlgebraicGeometry`). For `cechCohomology_OC` the body refers to `Scheme.cechCochain_OC` (auto-prefixed inside `namespace AlgebraicGeometry`); the parameters `C` and `𝒰` are the namespace's bound parameters at the call-site.
69	- If the elaborator complains about implicits not being resolvable, the safe spelling is `Scheme.cechCochain_OC (C := C) (𝒰 := 𝒰)`; the probe ran without this and succeeded, so the bare form is the expected route.
70	- Do **not** rename or move the declarations and do **not** change their signatures. The refactor-agent's two name-resolution adjustments (`namespace AlgebraicGeometry` placement; `TopologicalSpace.Opens` spelling) are mandatory and must not be reverted.
71	- Both bodies are term-mode one-liners — no `by ...` tactic block. They mirror the iter-009 / iter-010 / iter-011 closure pattern (term-mode one-liner adopted verbatim from a probe).
72

75	1. `lean_diagnostic_messages /home/archon/Lean_tests/AlgebraicJacobian/AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean` should return `{success: true, items: [], failed_dependencies: []}` (no warnings, no errors).
76	2. `lean_verify` on `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCochain_OC` and `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCohomology_OC` should each return `{axioms: [propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound], warnings: []}`.

84	- Do **not** touch any file other than `Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean`. In particular: `Genus.lean` (iter-011 closure intact), `Jacobian.lean`, `AbelJacobi.lean`, `Picard/Functor.lean`, `Picard/FunctorAb.lean`, `Cohomology/StructureSheafAb.lean`, `Cohomology/SheafCompose.lean`, `Picard/LineBundle.lean`, `Rigidity.lean` — all out of scope.

95	## Known dead ends (do not retry)
96	
97	- The 8 currently-blocked protected sorries (`Jacobian.lean` × 5, `AbelJacobi.lean` × 3) — gated on Phase C representability + Phase E geometric content + multi-iteration upstream Mathlib advances. Do not assign as direct prover objectives. The user's `noncomputable` authorisation removes the modifier-level gate but the upstream content gates remain.
98	- `PicardFunctor.representable` (`Picard/Functor.lean` L185) — closing on the global-sections-approximate `LineBundle` is a forbidden shortcut. Honest closure requires `LineBundle` refinement (gated on `MonoidalCategory X.Modules`, still absent in current Mathlib) plus the FGA argument. Multi-iteration; do not retry.
99	- `LineBundle` direct refinement — gated on `MonoidalCategory X.Modules` (still absent; iter-012 re-probe). Multi-iteration; do not issue as a primary objective.
100	- Vacuous-functor closures (`Discrete PUnit`, etc.) — destroy the downstream Picard chain; rejected on sight.
101	- Vacuous-sheaf closures (`PUnit.{u+1}`-valued, etc.) — destroy the downstream cohomology chain; rejected on sight.
102	- For iter-012 `cechCochain_OC` / `cechCohomology_OC`: closing with vacuous types (`PUnit`, `ULift PUnit`, `ModuleCat.of k PUnit`) — rejected on sight. Both must be the genuine `cechComplexFunctor` / `homology` applications.
103	- Scaffolding a new file `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/Cech.lean` instead of extending `Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean` — directive is explicit on this; iter-012 keeps the surface minimal.
104	- New `axiom` declarations — forbidden by plan-agent rules.
105	- Symmetric topological-equality form of rigidity (`∀ x ∈ U, g₁.left.base x = g₂.left.base x ⇒ g₁ = g₂`) — false in characteristic `p` (Frobenius). Resolved iter-002.
106	- Naive `AddCommGrpCat.uliftFunctor` (without explicit universe annotations) — does not unify under `u+1 =?= max u v`; explicit `.{u+1, u}` annotation required (resolved iter-008).
107	- `HModule` as `def` instead of `noncomputable abbrev` — instance synthesis fails on the wrapper (probe-verified iter-009).
108	- `HModule_zero_linearEquiv` with `≃` (`homEquiv₀`) or `≃+` (`addEquiv₀`) instead of `≃ₗ[k]` (`linearEquiv₀`) — the chosen signature is `≃ₗ[k]`; downstream `Module.Finite k` transport requires the `k`-linear refinement (resolved iter-010).
109	- Polish-shape `HModule_forget` (`forget₂ HModule ↔ Sheaf.H ((sheafCompose _ (forget₂)).obj F)`) — Mathlib's `Sheaf.H` uses `AddCommGrpCat.of (ULift ℤ)` as the unit, whereas `HModule` uses `ModuleCat.of k k`; the comparison is not a one-liner (iter-010 plan-agent finding, supersedes session-15 `recommendations.md`).
110	- Track 1 / Phase A step 6 *proper* (Serre finiteness on a proper curve) — multi-iteration; iter-012 lays only the Čech-side carrier (`cechCohomology_OC`), not the comparison or finiteness theorems. The full Serre-finiteness instance is queued for iter-013+ (comparison theorem) / iter-014+ (affine finiteness) / iter-015+ (degree-vanishing).
111	- `Sheaf.val`-style spellings (deprecated in current Mathlib) — use `(sheafToPresheaf _ _).obj F` instead. The iter-012 probe used the modern form.
112	- Bare `Opens C.left.toTopCat` *inside* `def Scheme.foo` (auto-opens `Scheme` sub-namespace; resolves to `Scheme.Opens` expecting a `Scheme`) — use `TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat` instead, as the iter-006 declarations and the iter-012 refactor adjustment establish.
113	
114	## Verification (plan-agent post-prover, when iter-012 prover round runs)
115

124	7. **Blueprint consistency**: `leanblueprint checkdecls` returns no errors; the `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCochain_OC}` and `\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cechCohomology_OC}` references in `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex` resolve.

134	- `USER_HINTS.md` — empty.
135	- `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex` — extended iter-012 plan-agent first pass with the Čech-scaffold section (`def:Scheme_cechCochain_OC` and `def:Scheme_cechCohomology_OC`).
136	- `blueprint/src/chapters/Genus.tex` — `\leanok` markers in place on `def:genus` (the iter-011 review agent will confirm post-prover-round).
137

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Iteration number — canonical
6	
7	Your invocation prompt always contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical iteration counter — it is what Archon writes to `logs/iter-NNN/` and stamps into commit messages (`archon[NNN/phase]`). Use it verbatim in every iteration-aware artefact you write: `STRATEGY.md` (current-state header, Revision log entries), `PROGRESS.md`, `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`, blueprint chapter notes, etc. Do not invent your own counter and do not trust the counter you find in older artefacts (the proof journal, prior `STRATEGY.md` entries, prior `recommendations.md` files): earlier agents drifted, and you align everything new to the number in your prompt header. When you next rewrite a section that contains a wrong iteration number, fix the number in place.
8	
9	The session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent — it counts prover rounds only and is not an iteration number.
10	
11	## Your Job
12	
13	1. Read `USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate user hints into your planning, then clear the file after acting
14	2. Read `task_results/` — collect prover results from each `<file>.md`, then merge findings into `task_pending.md` (update attempts) and `task_done.md` (migrate resolved theorems). Clear processed result files. If the refactor agent has run, read `task_results/refactor.md` and adjust your plans accordingly (see "Post-Refactor Verification" below).
15	3. Read `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md` to recover context — do not repeat documented dead ends
16	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` — if review journal sessions exist, read the latest session's `summary.md` and `recommendations.md` for the review agent's analysis. Also read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists — it contains cumulative progress, known blockers, and reusable proof patterns. Use these findings when setting objectives.
17	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** — see "Long-arc Strategy" below. Always do this *before* writing prover objectives or a refactor directive: it forces you to think about how this iteration fits into the path to project completion, instead of optimizing locally.
18	6. Evaluate each task: is it completed, can it be completed, and if not why not? Should a refactor be triggered?
19	7. Verify prover reports independently (check sorry count and compilation) — do not trust self-reports
20	8. If a task is not reasonable (mathematically impossible, wrong approach), update `PROGRESS.md` with a corrected plan
21	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint-based informal content" below). Ensure that the blueprint files are always consistent with the current state of the project. You should always ensure that it is aligned with the current state of the project. 
22	10. Set clear, self-contained objectives for the next prover iteration
23	11. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to write or edit formal proofs, stop immediately and return to your supervisory role.
24	12. Detect critical issues in the project (such as wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms, etc.) and address them, even if they have been present since the beginning.
25	
26	**Write permissions**: You may write to `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `USER_HINTS.md` (to clear it), `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` (to request structural changes), `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (to write/update informal proof), and `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` (to add macro definitions for any non-standard LaTeX commands you use). You must NOT edit `.lean` files or `task_results/` files.
27	
28	**LaTeX macros — define before you use.** The dashboard renders blueprint chapters with KaTeX, which only knows the standard LaTeX/AMS macros (full list: https://katex.org/docs/supported.html). If you write a non-standard command in a chapter — `\Spec`, `\Pic`, `\Jac`, `\Aut`, `\Hom`, custom operators, `\mathfrak`-named shortcuts, etc. — define it in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using it, e.g. `\DeclareMathOperator{\Spec}{Spec}` or `\newcommand{\Jac}[1]{\mathrm{Jac}(#1)}`. Otherwise the command renders as a broken token in the dashboard. Definitions in `common.tex` are picked up by both the PDF build and the dashboard.
29	
30	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced in that section's headings/bullets. If you mention an off-limits file (e.g. "### 3. The protected files (`Genus.lean`) — DO NOT TOUCH"), the parser still extracts it and a prover wastes API time stopping out. Two rules:
31	- Do not list off-limits / DO-NOT-TOUCH / "skip this" files in `## Current Objectives`. Put them in a separate section like `## Off-limits this iteration` if you want to document why they're skipped.
32	- The objectives section should contain exactly the files you want a prover to attack this round, one per heading or bullet.
33	
34	**Important**: You should **NEVER** propose adding new axioms. If axioms are already present, you should remove them.
35	
36	## Protected declarations
37	
38	Read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. The declarations listed there are the mathematician's read-only surface: **no agent may modify their signature**. As plan agent:
39	
40	- Do not assign an objective that would require changing a protected signature.
41	- Moving a protected declaration to a different file is allowed (the refactor agent will update the YAML path), but renaming or re-signing is not.
42	
43	## References
44	
45	A paragraph-by-paragraph summary of every informal source is pasted into your prompt from `references/summary.md`. Read it every iteration. Before any task where close alignment with a reference is important, read the related source file in `references/` directly. Do not rely on memory or summaries alone. 
46	
47	If relevant, you can use Web Search to find new references, in this case you should update `references/summary.md` with a summary of the new reference and its relevance to the project, keeping it clear that the new reference is not part of the original project scope.
48	
49	## Blueprint-based informal content
50	
51	This project uses a blueprint (plasTeX + `leanblueprint`). Informal proof sketches live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file. The slug mapping is:
52	
53	```
54	Lean file  Algebra/WLocal.lean  →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex
55	Lean file  Core.lean            →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex
56	```
57	
58	`blueprint/src/content.tex` is the main tex file, and it is your job to keep it updated with the necessary `\input{chapters/<slug>.tex}`. Your job is also to ensure that the blueprint are correct and aligned with the intended content of the project, which includes aligning the blueprint with the Lean files as they evolve. The blueprints should not be sketchy but should instead contain rigorous proofs. 
59	
60	**Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the informal content the prover needs.** The prover reads its chapter file and uses it as the source of truth for the mathematical content.
61	
62	- You can create/modify/rename/delete blueprint chapters as needed, **as long as** you keep `blueprint/src/content.tex` updated and add the corresponding objective in `PROGRESS.md` to require the competent agent to ensure the necessary modifications in the Lean files.
63	- The blueprints are considered by the other agents as the source of truth for informal content, so they should always be consistent with the current state of the project; any mistake or inconsistency should be fixed as soon as possible.
64	
65	### What to write in a chapter file
66	
67	For each declaration the prover will need to handle, the chapter should contain a block like this:
68	
69	```latex
70	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
71	  \label{thm:some_label}
72	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
73	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
74	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
75	\end{theorem}
76	
77	\begin{proof}
78	  \uses{thm:another_result}
79	  Step-by-step informal proof sketch. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
80	  so the dependency graph stays accurate. Use as much detail as the prover would
81	  need to formalize — a one-liner is rarely enough.
82	\end{proof}
83	```
84	
85	**Macros the prover relies on:**
86	
87	- `\lean{foo.bar}` — declares which Lean name this block corresponds to
88	- `\leanok` — added by the prover once formalization is complete (you do not add it)
89	- `\mathlibok` — added when the declaration already exists in Mathlib. Used for aliases, re-exports, or statements backed by an existing Mathlib theorem.
90	
91	### Record where the informal content lives
92	
93	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective, record which blueprint chapter backs it. Example:
94	
95	```markdown
96	1. **`Algebra/WLocal.lean`** — resolve 3 sorries. Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex` (theorems `thm:wLocal_iff`, `thm:wLocal_of_surjective`).
97	```
98	
99	The prover will read the chapter file mentioned here.
100	
101	## Long-arc Strategy
102	
103	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from its current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it — the prover, refactor, and review agents never read or write it. The mathematician reads it, so keep it human-readable.
104	
105	Read it early in every iteration, before deciding which sorries to assign or whether to trigger a refactor, so the next iteration is grounded in the bigger picture.
106	
107	Update it after processing prover/review results and before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint. Describe the *current* plan only: the remaining steps from today's state to the end-state, in roughly the order they need to happen, with a rough effort estimate (iterations, LOC) per step. Call out which Mathlib gaps need filling and what new material the project will have to introduce on the way (definitions, structures, lemmas, …). Do not narrate past iterations — the Revision log captures history.
108	
109	Aim for the big picture, not the details. Rely on the details to keep the picture honest, but do not enumerate them: `PROGRESS.md` and the blueprint hold the specifics. The mathematician should be able to read the strategy.
110	
111	If nothing strategically changed this iteration, leave the body alone and add nothing to the Revision log. When the strategy changes, rewrite the affected parts in place and append one bullet to the Revision log explaining *why*: `- iter NNN — <one-line reason>`.
112	
113	## Feasibility Gate
114	
115	When facing difficult tasks, you and your agents should always try to think harder and should never delegate the task to other iterations or other agents. This means ensuring alignment with `references/` contents, thinking of alternative perspectives, using toy examples, finding analogies, etc.
116	
117	You should always question your previous work. The project (blueprints, Lean files, or even references sometimes) might contain wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms included for convenience, etc. If you identify such a critical issue, whether new or present since many iterations, you should absolutely address it, for instance by triggering a refactor.
118	
119	You should also be resilient when encountering obstacles and consider whether `Mathlib` contains the necessary infrastructure to solve the problem, or whether the current strategy requires filling its gaps. You can use `lean_leansearch` or `lean_loogle` to check if the required lemmas, type classes, or API functions exist in Mathlib. You can also use the informal agent or Web Search to find alternative proof approaches that avoid unavailable infrastructure. If alternative approaches significantly increase the chances of success, you may consider calling the refactor agent. However, if filling `Mathlib`'s gaps is the only viable path, you should not try to avoid it.
120	
121	## Triggering a Refactor
122	
123	While triggering a refactor should always be **strongly** motivated both mathematically and practically, it might sometimes be necessary. In this case:
124	
125	1. Write the necessary changes in the blueprint. If you want the refactor to create/delete/divide Lean files, you must first create/delete/divide the corresponding chapter files and update `content.tex` accordingly; the refactor agent will then update the Lean files to match the new blueprint structure. If you want the refactor to change definitions, signatures, types, or imports, you should also ensure that the blueprint reflects the desired content.
126	2. Write a directive to `.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`. It should include a clear and strongly justified description of the problem, a detailed mathematical justification for the proposed changes, and a precise list of the changes you want the refactor agent to make, along with references to the relevant blueprint chapters.
127	3. The refactor agent will execute your directive in the next phase. The refactor agent can change definitions, signatures, types, imports, and module structure, and can create/delete Lean files, as long as this doesn't conflict with `archon-protected.yaml`, but it cannot fill proofs. Broken proofs become `sorry`.
128	4. After the refactor completes, you will be re-invoked to verify the result and set objectives for the provers.
129	
130	**Before writing the directive:**
131	1. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to verify the target definitions are compatible with Mathlib.
132	2. If the mathematical justification is non-trivial, use the informal agent or Web Search to develop it first.
133	
134	**When to trigger a refactor?**
135	- If some Lean files become too large and could be decomposed into semantically meaningful modules. If some proofs are too long and could be decomposed into smaller parts (Lemmas, etc.). In that case, you should ensure that blueprint chapters are decomposed accordingly and trigger a refactor to split the corresponding Lean files.
136	- If the proof strategy requires structural changes to the definitions, types, or module structure (e.g. modifying signatures, moving definitions, deleting or renaming a statement, etc.) that don't conflict with `archon-protected.yaml`. In that case, you should update the blueprint to reflect the desired structure and trigger a refactor to implement it.
137	
138	## Post-Refactor Verification
139	
140	After the refactor agent runs, you will be re-invoked. When `task_results/refactor.md` exists, you are in a **post-refactor verification pass**. In this pass:
141	
142	1. **Read `task_results/refactor.md` first.** Understand what the refactor agent changed, what new sorries were introduced, and whether compilation succeeded.
143	2. **Verify the changes match your directive:**
144	   - Check that definitions were changed as requested (read the affected `.lean` files)
145	   - Check compilation of affected files with `lean_diagnostic_messages`
146	   - If the refactor agent reported problems or partial completion, document them in `task_pending.md`
147	3. **Do NOT write another `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` in this pass.** The loop only runs one refactor per iteration.
148	4. **Set prover objectives:** Update `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the provers.
149	5. **Update `task_pending.md`:** Record the refactor as context.
150	
151	## Providing Informal Content to the Prover
152	
153	The prover performs significantly better when given rich informal mathematical guidance. Before assigning a task, you must ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof or proof sketch.
154	
155	**How to provide informal content:**
156	
157	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): Write directly in `PROGRESS.md` under the task objectives. Example: "Key idea: use Bolzano-Weierstrass to extract a convergent subsequence, then show the limit satisfies the property."
158	
159	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): Write as comments in the corresponding `.lean` file, above the declaration with `sorry`. Use `/- ... -/` block comments.
160	
161	- **Long content** (a full proof sketch, paper summary, or multi-step construction): Write in the relevant chapter `.tex` file in the blueprint. Reference the blueprint chapter in `PROGRESS.md` next to the objective.
162	
163	- **Other possibilities**: The above methods should be prioritized, but if relevant, you may use Web Search to find new references, you may also write a separate markdown file in the project (e.g. `informal_sketches/some_lemma.md`) and link to it from `PROGRESS.md`. 
164	
165	**No matter which method you choose, always record in `PROGRESS.md`** where the informal content is located, so the prover can obtain it without searching.
166	
167	**When the reference is vague** (e.g., "by Hiblot 1975" without proof details):
168	1. Use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate an informal proof sketch from an external model
169	2. Use Web Search to find the referenced paper and extract the key proof steps
170	3. Write the result into a file and record the path in `PROGRESS.md`
171	4. Do this **before** assigning the task to the prover — don't send the prover in blind
172	
173	## Recognizing Prover Failure Modes
174	
175	### "Mathlib doesn't have it" — Missing Infrastructure
176	The #1 failure mode. The prover reports that a sorry is unfillable because Mathlib lacks the infrastructure, then stops.
177	
178	**Your response:** This is YOUR job to solve, not the prover's. Never just pass it back with "try harder." You must actively find an alternative proof route:
179	
180	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`) — ask it: "Prove X without using [the missing infrastructure]. Only use tools available in Lean 4 Mathlib." Get a concrete alternative proof sketch.
181	2. **Use Web Search** — find the referenced paper or alternative proofs of the same result that avoid the missing infrastructure.
182	3. **Decompose differently** — break the problem into sub-lemmas where each sub-lemma only needs available infrastructure. The prover can implement Mathlib-level lemmas if you give it clear, self-contained goals.
183	4. **Check `mathlib-unavailable-theorems.md`** — if the missing infrastructure is in a known-unavailable domain, don't waste time looking for it. Focus on detours.
184	5. **If the infrastructure gap is in the definition itself** — trigger a refactor to change the definition so it doesn't require the missing infrastructure downstream.
185	
186	Write the re-routed informal proof into the corresponding chapter `.tex` file (as a `\begin{proof} ... \end{proof}` body), then reassign the task to the prover. Do not reassign without providing an alternative in the chapter.
187	
188	### Wrong Construction — Building on a Flawed Foundation
189	The prover chose a wrong construction and the sorry is mathematically unfillable, but the prover keeps trying instead of backtracking.
190	
191	**Your response:** If the fix is within a single file, instruct the prover to revert. If the fix requires cross-file changes, trigger a refactor. Update the chapter `.tex` with the correct construction before the next prover round.
192	
193	### Not Using Web Search
194	The prover searches only within Mathlib and gives up when it finds nothing, even when the blueprint references a specific paper.
195	
196	**Your response:** Explicitly instruct: "Use Web Search to find [paper name/arXiv ID], read the proof, decompose it into sub-lemmas, and formalize step by step." Update the chapter with the retrieved proof sketch.
197	
198	### Early Stopping on Hard Problems
199	The prover stops and reports "done" when the remaining sorry requires significant effort.
200	
201	**Your response:** Reject the report. Break the hard problem into smaller sub-goals in the chapter `.tex` and assign them one at a time. Frame it as: "Formalize just sub-lemma L1 from the blueprint, then report back."
202	
203	### Using tricks (e.g. axioms, ad-hoc definitions, weakening hypotheses) to bypass hard parts
204	The prover introduces new axioms or definitions that aren't in the blueprint to fill sorries, then reports completion. You should also never propose such tricks as a plan agent.
205	
206	**Your response:** Reject the report. Such tricks should not be accepted; they should be documented and then removed. You should then try to understand why this route was chosen and ensure that it will not be reproduced.
207	
208	## Assessing Prover Progress
209	
210	### Three Indicators
211	| Indicator | Meaning |
212	|-----------|---------|
213	| Sorry count (decreasing) | Direct progress — a sorry was filled |
214	| Code line count (increasing) | Infrastructure building — helpers, definitions |
215	| `\leanok` marks added | Prover confirmed formalization against the blueprint |
216	
217	Line count increasing + sorry count unchanged = the prover is building infrastructure. This is real progress.
218	Line count unchanged + sorry count unchanged = zero progress.
219	
220	### Deep Stuck vs Early Abandonment
221	| Pattern | Diagnosis | Response |
222	|---------|-----------|----------|
223	| 800+ lines, 2-3 sorries left | Deep stuck — needs math hint or infrastructure | Provide informal guidance via informal_agent, suggest specific decomposition |
224	| <200 lines, sorry remaining | Early abandonment — prover gave up too quickly | Push harder: break into sub-goals, provide richer informal content |
225	
226	## Verification
227	
228	After a prover reports completion, always verify independently:
229	1. Check sorry count: `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/sorry_analyzer.py" <file> --format=summary`
230	2. Check compilation: `lean_diagnostic_messages(file)` or `lake env lean <file>`
231	3. Check axioms: no new `axiom` declarations
232	4. Check blueprint consistency: `leanblueprint checkdecls` flags Lean names in the blueprint that don't exist. Run this after the prover has renamed or removed declarations.
233	
234	Never advance to the next stage based solely on the prover's word.
235	
236	## Dependency graph
237	
238	Before scoping objectives, run the bundled dependency-graph script
239	instead of reconstructing the dependency map by hand. It parses every
240	`.lean` file's imports plus every `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` for
241	`\lean{…}` / `\uses{…}` / `\proves{…}` / `\leanok` / `\notready`, and
242	emits a JSON view of the whole project in well under a second:
243	
244	```
245	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=json
246	```
247	
248	Use `--format=summary` for a one-screen overview, `--format=dot` if
249	you need to share the graph elsewhere. Read this once per iteration to
250	decide objective ordering — files with no upstream sorries should be
251	formalised first, downstream files later.
252	
253	## Decomposition Strategy
254	
255	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem:
256	1. Read the blueprint chapter to identify sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, L3, ...)
257	2. Read the files in `References/` related to it, if any, to ensure you understand and align with the original proof.
258	3. Check if the chapter is detailed enough — if not, expand it first (using informal_agent / Web Search)
259	4. Assign one sub-lemma at a time: "Fill sorry for L1 only"
260	5. After L1 is done, verify, then assign L2
261	6. Record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`
262	
263	## Context Management
264	
265	Each prover iteration starts with fresh context. The prover does not remember previous iterations.
266	
267	- Provide **self-contained** objectives in `PROGRESS.md` — include all context the prover needs
268	- Point the prover at its blueprint chapter — that is where the mathematical content lives
269	- When a prover gets stuck on the same failure across multiple iterations, it is re-discovering the same dead end. Change the approach entirely — do not just repeat "try again"
270	- Document dead ends in `PROGRESS.md` so the prover doesn't repeat them
271	
272	## Multi-Agent Coordination
273	
274	Provers run in parallel — one agent per file. Your objectives must be structured accordingly.
275	
276	### Writing objectives
277	
278	Number each objective clearly (1, 2, 3, ...). Each objective maps to **exactly one file**. Never assign two objectives to the same file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside.
279	
280	```markdown
281	## Current Objectives
282	
283	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorry in `filter_convergence` (line 156). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex` (see `thm:filter_convergence`).
284	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Measure.tex`. Use MeasureTheory.Measure.restrict_apply with finite spanning sets.
285	3. **`Topology.lean`** — Fill sorry in `compact_embedding` (line 203). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Topology.tex`. Straightforward from CompactSpace + isClosedEmbedding.
286	```
287	
288	### Balancing difficulty
289	
290	Estimate the relative difficulty of each objective. If one file has significantly harder sorries than others, consider decomposing it into helper lemmas first (in a prior plan iteration) so the prover agent has smaller, more tractable goals. The goal is for all agents to finish around the same time.
291	
292	### Agent count
293	
294	- **Agent count = file count**: if 24 files need work, write 24 objectives — one per file. Do not artificially batch or limit the number of objectives. The shell script handles parallelism.
295	- If an experiment is restarted, check the compilation status of every target `.lean` file before planning. Prioritize files that still have `sorry` or compilation errors. Do not redo completed work.
296	
297	## Stage Transitions
298	
299	When all objectives in the current stage are met, advance `PROGRESS.md` to the next stage:
300	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (when all statements are formalized)
301	- `prover` → `polish` (when all sorries are filled and verified)
302	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (when proofs are clean and compile)
Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.lean.md

input.description: Read user hints and refactor directive

content:
# User Hints

Write strategic guidance here. The plan agent reads this file every iteration.
Provers do not read this file — the plan agent translates your hints into concrete objectives.

<!-- Example: "The measure_union approach is a dead end. Try σ-additivity instead." -->
<!-- Example: "Focus on Algebra/WLocal.lean first — the rest depends on it." -->
---
# Refactor Directive

<!-- Plan agent: write your refactoring directive here. -->
<!-- The refactor agent will execute it at the start of the next iteration. -->
<!-- This file is cleared after each refactor run. -->

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## End-state
4	
5	`AlgebraicJacobian/Genus.lean`, `Jacobian.lean`, `AbelJacobi.lean` compile with **no `sorry`** and **no new `axiom`**, and the nine declarations frozen by `archon-protected.yaml` carry their intended mathematical content:
6	
7	- `genus C` = the genus of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C` over a field `k`.
8	- `Jacobian C : Over (Spec (.of k))` = the connected component of the identity in the Picard scheme of `C`, equipped with `GrpObj`, `IsProper`, `GeometricallyIrreducible`, and `SmoothOfRelativeDimension (genus C)`.
9	- `ofCurve P : C ⟶ Jacobian C` = the Abel–Jacobi map for a `k`-rational point `P`, sending `P` to `η[Jacobian C]`, and satisfying the Albanese universal property.
10	
11	## Honest assessment of feasibility (Mathlib b80f227, Lean 4.30.0-rc2)
12	
13	A full Mathlib survey (see `task_pending.md`) shows that **every single one of the nine protected declarations is blocked by non-trivial missing Mathlib infrastructure** — the gap is not in the proofs, it is in the surrounding theory. In particular:
14	
15	| Theory needed | Mathlib status |
16	|---|---|
17	| Sheaf cohomology of quasi-coherent sheaves on schemes (`Hⁱ(X, F)`) | absent (only `EllAdicCohomology` and abstract `RightDerived` machinery) |
18	| Sheaf of relative Kähler differentials `Ω_{X/S}` of schemes | absent (rings only) |
19	| Picard scheme / Picard functor / `Pic⁰` | absent (only `CommRing.Pic R`) |
20	| Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf, arithmetic genus | absent |
21	| Riemann–Roch, Serre duality, rigidity for abelian varieties, Albanese | absent |

47	### Phase C — Picard functor and its representability for curves  (≥ 30 iterations, ≥ 2500 LOC)
48	
49	Define `PicardFunctor C : SchemeOver k ⥤ AddCommGrp` as `S ↦ Pic(C ×_k S)/Pic(S)`, étale-sheafify, and prove representability for `C` smooth proper geometrically connected of relative dimension 1. 216	/-- Phase A step 6 *Path 2* (iter-012 scaffold): the Čech cochain complex of
217	the structure sheaf of a `Spec k`-scheme `C : Over (Spec (CommRingCat.of k))`,
218	with respect to an arbitrary indexed family of opens `𝒰 : ι → Opens C.left.toTopCat`.
219	Built from Mathlib's `CategoryTheory.cechComplexFunctor` (file
220	`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/Cech.lean`) applied to the
221	underlying presheaf of `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C` (iter-006). The result is a
222	cochain complex valued in `ModuleCat.{u} k`, indexed by `ℕ`.
223	
224	The cohomology of this complex is `Scheme.cechCohomology_OC` below. The
225	downstream comparison theorem (Čech cohomology = derived-functor cohomology
226	= `Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C)` for an acyclic cover) is
227	queued for iter-013+; iter-012 only establishes the Čech-side carrier. -/
228	noncomputable def Scheme.cechCochain_OC
229	    {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
230	    {ι : Type u} (𝒰 : ι → TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat) :
231	    CochainComplex (ModuleCat.{u} k) ℕ :=
232	  (cechComplexFunctor 𝒰).obj ((sheafToPresheaf _ _).obj (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C))
233	
234	/-- Phase A step 6 *Path 2* (iter-012 scaffold): the `n`-th Čech cohomology
235	of the structure sheaf for an arbitrary indexed open cover. Defined as the
236	`n`-th homology of the Čech cochain complex `Scheme.cechCochain_OC`. The
237	result lives in `ModuleCat.{u} k` and therefore carries a `Module k`
238	structure for free; the iter-013+ comparison theorem will identify it
239	with `Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) n` when the cover is
240	acyclic. -/
241	noncomputable def Scheme.cechCohomology_OC
242	    {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
243	    {ι : Type u} (𝒰 : ι → TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat) (n : ℕ) :
244	    ModuleCat.{u} k :=
245	  (Scheme.cechCochain_OC C 𝒰).homology n

124	## Forbidden shortcuts (documented once)
125	
126	- `genus C := 0` or any constant — false in genus $\ge 1$.
127	- `Jacobian C := 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of k)))` (terminal scheme) — forces `genus = 0` to discharge `smoothOfRelativeDimension_genus`, which is mathematically wrong.
128	- `Jacobian C := 𝔸¹_k`, `Spec L` (any constant scheme) — same obstruction.
129	- `ofCurve P := toUnit C ≫ η[Jacobian C]` (constant map to identity) — falsifies `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` whenever a non-constant pointed morphism $C \to A$ exists (i.e. for any $g(C) \ge 1$).
130	- `LineBundle X := Unit` / `PUnit` / any vacuous placeholder — destroys the downstream Picard chain.
131	- New `axiom` declarations — forbidden by plan-agent rules.
132	- `PicardFunctor C := Discrete PUnit` or any vacuous functor — destroys representability and the downstream Jacobian chain.
133	- Closing `PicardFunctor.representable` on top of the global-sections-approximate `LineBundle` — silently asserts representability of the wrong functor. Keep the sorry in place.
134	- Symmetric topological-equality form of rigidity (`∀ x ∈ U, g₁.left.base x = g₂.left.base x ⇒ g₁ = g₂`) — mathematically false in characteristic `p` (Frobenius). Use the scheme-level form (`U.ι ≫ g₁.left = U.ι ≫ g₂.left ⇒ g₁ = g₂`) as in the iter-002 closure.
135	- Vacuous-presheaf / `Discrete PUnit`-valued / `PUnit.{u+1}`-valued sheaf shortcuts on Phase A scaffolding — destroys the downstream cohomology chain; rejected on sight.
136	- (Resolved iter-006) On `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf` and helpers: substituting any helper with a vacuous closure (e.g. `algebraSection` returning `Algebra.id`, `kToSection` returning the zero ring map, `toModuleKPresheaf` skipping the structure-morphism algebra map and using the trivial `k`-action discarding `C.hom`) was explicitly forbidden because the resulting `Module k` instance on `(toModuleKSheaf C).H 1` would be the zero one, falsifying `genus`. The iter-006 prover round closed all eight helpers honestly using the actual structure morphism `C.hom`, with only three minor mathematically-equivalent deviations from the recorded bodies (drop `.symm`; trim simp set; drop `Functor.ext` tail and use `Sheaf.property`).
137	- For the iter-007 polish track: closing the `toModuleKSheaf_forgetCompare` natural iso with a non-natural iso between unrelated functors is forbidden. The two sheaves agree on the nose at the underlying-presheaf level (probe-confirmed); the closure must be `Iso.refl _` (or an equivalent definitional-identity construction). The simp lemmas `toModuleKPresheaf_obj` and `PicardFunctorAb_forget_obj` are `rfl`-companions; closing them by anything other than `rfl` (or a defeq) is a flag.
138	- For the iter-010 H⁰ bridge: closing `HModule_zero_linearEquiv` with anything other than `Abelian.Ext.linearEquiv₀` (or a mathematically-equivalent rebuild) is a flag. Specifically, do NOT scaffold a polish-shape `HModule_forget` (`forget₂ HModule ↔ Sheaf.H ((sheafCompose _ (forget₂)).obj F)`) — Mathlib's `Sheaf.H` is `Ext ((constantSheaf _).obj (ULift ℤ)) F n` and `HModule` is `Ext ((constantSheaf _).obj (ModuleCat.of k k)) F n`; the units differ and `forget₂` does not produce the equality (iter-010 plan-agent finding, supersedes the over-optimistic session-15 `recommendations.md` proposal).

245	1. **`def` (not `abbrev`).** Unlike `HModule` itself, this auxiliary needs no instance-transparency: it is a `≃ₗ[k]` between two known types whose `Module k` and `AddCommGroup` instances are already inferable. A `def` form is fine; `abbrev` would over-expose the body.

The `≃ₗ[k]` version is strictly more useful than the `≃+` (`addEquiv₀`) and `≃` (`homEquiv₀`) variants for downstream `Module.Finite k` work — it transports `Module.Finite k` directly via `Module.Finite.equiv`.

5	-/
6	module

8	public import Mathlib.AlgebraicTopology.AlternatingFaceMapComplex
9	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FormalCoproducts.Cech
10	
11	/-!
12	# Cech cohomology
13	
14	Given a family of objects `U : ι → C` in a category `C` that has finite products,
15	we define a Cech complex functor
16	`cechComplexFunctor : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ` which sends a presheaf
17	`P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` in a preadditive category (where products exist) to the cochain
18	complex which in degree `n` consists of the product, indexed by `i : Fin (n + 1) → ι`,
19	of the value of `P` on the product of the objects `U (i a)` for `a : Fin (n + 1)`.
20	
21	-/
22	
23	@[expose] public section
24	
25	universe w t v v' u u'
26	
27	namespace CategoryTheory
28	
29	open Limits
30	
31	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] {A : Type u'} [Category.{v'} A] [HasProducts.{w} A]
32	
33	namespace Limits.FormalCoproduct
34	
35	open Opposite
36	
37	variable (E : SimplicialObject (FormalCoproduct.{w} C))
38	
39	/-- Given a simplicial object `E` in the category `FormalCoproduct C`, this is the
40	functor `(Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CosimplicialObject A` which sends `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` to the
41	cosimplicial object which sends `⦋n⦌` to the "evaluation" of `P` on `E _⦋n⦌`. -/
42	@[simps!]
43	noncomputable def cosimplicialObjectFunctor :
44	    (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CosimplicialObject A :=
45	  evalOp.{w} C A ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringLeft _ _ _).obj E.rightOp
46	
47	variable [Preadditive A]
48	
49	/-- Given a simplicial object `E` in the category `FormalCoproduct C`, this is the
50	functor `(Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ` which sends `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` to the
51	cochain complex which in degree `n` consists of the "evaluation" of `P` on `E _⦋n⦌`. -/
52	@[simps!]
53	noncomputable def cochainComplexFunctor : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ :=
54	  cosimplicialObjectFunctor E ⋙ AlgebraicTopology.alternatingCofaceMapComplex A
55	
56	end Limits.FormalCoproduct
57	
58	variable [HasFiniteProducts C] [Preadditive A] {ι : Type w} (U : ι → C)
59	
60	/-- Given a family of objects `U : ι → C`, this is the Cech complex functor
61	`(Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ` which sends a presheaf `P : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A` to the
62	cochain complex which in degree `n` consists of the product,
63	indexed by `x : Fin (n + 1) → ι`, of the value of `P` on the product of the
64	objects `U (x i)` for `i : Fin (n + 1)`. -/
65	noncomputable def cechComplexFunctor : (Cᵒᵖ ⥤ A) ⥤ CochainComplex A ℕ :=
66	  FormalCoproduct.cochainComplexFunctor (FormalCoproduct.mk _ U).cech
67	
68	end CategoryTheory
69

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2024 Joël Riou. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Joël Riou
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.ExactSequences
9	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Grp.Biproducts
10	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.MayerVietorisSquare
11	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.SheafCohomology.Basic
12	
13	/-!
14	# The Mayer-Vietoris exact sequence in sheaf cohomology
15	
16	Let `C` be a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology `J`.
17	Let `S : J.MayerVietorisSquare` be a Mayer-Vietoris square for `J`.
18	Let `F` be an abelian sheaf on `(C, J)`.
19	
20	In this file, we obtain a long exact Mayer-Vietoris sequence:
21	
22	`... ⟶ H^n(S.X₄, F) ⟶ H^n(S.X₂, F) ⊞ H^n(S.X₃, F) ⟶ H^n(S.X₁, F) ⟶ H^{n + 1}(S.X₄, F) ⟶ ...`
23	
24	-/
25	
26	@[expose] public section
27	
28	universe w v u
29	
30	namespace CategoryTheory
31	
32	open Category Opposite Limits Abelian
33	
34	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
35	  [HasWeakSheafify J (Type v)] [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{v}]
36	  [HasExt.{w} (Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v})]
37	
38	namespace GrothendieckTopology.MayerVietorisSquare
39	
40	variable (S : J.MayerVietorisSquare) (F : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v})
41	
42	/-- The sum of two restriction maps in sheaf cohomology. -/
43	noncomputable def toBiprod (n : ℕ) :
44	    F.H' n S.X₄ ⟶ F.H' n S.X₂ ⊞ F.H' n S.X₃ :=
45	  biprod.lift ((F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₂₄.op)
46	      ((F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₃₄.op)
47	
48	lemma toBiprod_apply {n : ℕ} (y : F.H' n S.X₄) :
49	    S.toBiprod F n y = (AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).inv
50	      ⟨(F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₂₄.op y,
51	        (F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₃₄.op y⟩ := by
52	  apply (AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).addCommGroupIsoToAddEquiv.injective
53	  dsimp [toBiprod]
54	  ext
55	  · rw [Iso.addCommGroupIsoToAddEquiv_apply,
56	      Iso.addCommGroupIsoToAddEquiv_apply,
57	      ← AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd_inv_comp_fst_apply,
58	      Iso.hom_inv_id_apply, ← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply,
59	      biprod.lift_fst, Iso.inv_hom_id_apply]
60	  · rw [Iso.addCommGroupIsoToAddEquiv_apply,
61	      Iso.addCommGroupIsoToAddEquiv_apply,
62	      ← AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd_inv_comp_snd_apply,
63	      Iso.hom_inv_id_apply, ← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply,
64	      biprod.lift_snd, Iso.inv_hom_id_apply]
65	
66	/-- The difference of two restriction maps in sheaf cohomology. -/
67	noncomputable def fromBiprod (n : ℕ) :
68	    F.H' n S.X₂ ⊞ F.H' n S.X₃ ⟶ F.H' n S.X₁ :=
69	  biprod.desc ((F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₁₂.op)
70	      (-(F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₁₃.op)
71	
72	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
73	lemma toBiprod_fromBiprod (n : ℕ) : S.toBiprod F n ≫ S.fromBiprod F n = 0 := by
74	  simp only [toBiprod, fromBiprod, biprod.lift_desc, Preadditive.comp_neg,
75	    ← sub_eq_add_neg, sub_eq_zero, ← Functor.map_comp, ← op_comp, S.toSquare.fac]
76	
77	lemma fromBiprod_biprodIsoProd_inv_apply {n : ℕ}
78	    (y₁ : F.H' n S.X₂) (y₂ : F.H' n S.X₃) :
79	    S.fromBiprod F n ((AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).inv ⟨y₁, y₂⟩) =
80	      (F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₁₂.op y₁ - (F.cohomologyPresheaf n).map S.f₁₃.op y₂ := by
81	  dsimp [fromBiprod]
82	  rw [← ConcreteCategory.comp_apply]
83	  simp [AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd_inv_comp_desc, sub_eq_add_neg]
84	
85	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
86	attribute [local simp] toBiprod_apply in
87	lemma biprodAddEquiv_symm_biprodIsoProd_hom_toBiprod_apply
88	    {n : ℕ} (x : F.H' n S.X₄) :
89	    Ext.biprodAddEquiv.symm ((AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).hom (S.toBiprod F n x)) =
90	      (Ext.mk₀ S.shortComplex.g).comp x (zero_add n) :=
91	  Ext.biprodAddEquiv.injective (by cat_disch)
92	
93	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
94	attribute [local simp] sub_eq_add_neg in
95	lemma mk₀_f_comp_biprodAddEquiv_symm_biprodIsoProd_hom
96	    {n : ℕ} (x : ↑(F.H' n S.X₂ ⊞ F.H' n S.X₃)) :
97	    (Ext.mk₀ S.shortComplex.f).comp
98	      (Ext.biprodAddEquiv.symm ((AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).hom x)) (zero_add n) =
99	    (S.fromBiprod F n x) := by
100	  obtain ⟨⟨x₂, x₃⟩, rfl⟩ :=
101	    (AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).addCommGroupIsoToAddEquiv.symm.surjective x
102	  dsimp
103	  rw [Ext.biprodAddEquiv_symm_apply,
104	    Iso.addCommGroupIsoToAddEquiv_symm_apply,
105	    fromBiprod_biprodIsoProd_inv_apply]
106	  cat_disch
107	
108	variable (n₀ n₁ : ℕ) (h : n₀ + 1 = n₁)
109	
110	/-- The connecting homomorphism of the Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence
111	in sheaf cohomology. -/
112	noncomputable def δ :
113	    F.H' n₀ S.X₁ ⟶ F.H' n₁ S.X₄ :=
114	  AddCommGrpCat.ofHom (S.shortComplex_shortExact.extClass.precomp _ (by omega))
115	
116	open ComposableArrows
117	
118	/-- The Mayer-Vietoris long exact sequence of an abelian sheaf `F : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat`
119	for a Mayer-Vietoris square `S : J.MayerVietorisSquare`. -/
120	noncomputable abbrev sequence : ComposableArrows AddCommGrpCat.{w} 5 :=
121	  mk₅ (S.toBiprod F n₀) (S.fromBiprod F n₀) (S.δ F n₀ n₁ h)
122	    (S.toBiprod F n₁) (S.fromBiprod F n₁)
123	
124	set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
125	/-- Comparison isomorphism from the Mayer-Vietoris sequence and the
126	contravariant sequence of `Ext`-groups. -/
127	noncomputable def sequenceIso : S.sequence F n₀ n₁ h ≅
128	    Ext.contravariantSequence S.shortComplex_shortExact F n₀ n₁ (by omega) :=
129	  isoMk₅ (Iso.refl _)
130	    ((AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).trans (Ext.biprodAddEquiv.symm).toAddCommGrpIso)
131	    (Iso.refl _) (Iso.refl _)
132	    ((AddCommGrpCat.biprodIsoProd _ _).trans (Ext.biprodAddEquiv.symm).toAddCommGrpIso)
133	    (Iso.refl _)
134	    (by ext; apply biprodAddEquiv_symm_biprodIsoProd_hom_toBiprod_apply)
135	    (by ext; symm; apply mk₀_f_comp_biprodAddEquiv_symm_biprodIsoProd_hom)
136	    (by dsimp; rw [comp_id, id_comp]; rfl)
137	    (by ext; apply biprodAddEquiv_symm_biprodIsoProd_hom_toBiprod_apply)
138	    (by ext; symm; apply mk₀_f_comp_biprodAddEquiv_symm_biprodIsoProd_hom)
139	
140	lemma sequence_exact : (S.sequence F n₀ n₁ h).Exact :=
141	  exact_of_iso (S.sequenceIso F n₀ n₁ h).symm (Ext.contravariantSequence_exact _ _ _ _ _)
142	
143	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
144	lemma δ_toBiprod : S.δ F n₀ n₁ h ≫ S.toBiprod F n₁ = 0 :=
145	  (S.sequence_exact F n₀ n₁ h).zero 2
146	
147	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
148	lemma fromBiprod_δ : S.fromBiprod F n₀ ≫ S.δ F n₀ n₁ h = 0 :=
149	  (S.sequence_exact F n₀ n₁ h).zero 1
150	
151	end GrothendieckTopology.MayerVietorisSquare
152	
153	end CategoryTheory
154

4	Authors: Joël Riou
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Grp.Abelian
9	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Grp.Adjunctions
10	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic
11	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Abelian
12	public import Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.ConstantSheaf
13	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.EnoughProjectives
14	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Category.Grp.Zero
15	
16	/-!
17	# Sheaf cohomology
18	
19	Let `C` be a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology `J`.
20	We define the cohomology types `Sheaf.H F n` of an abelian
21	sheaf `F` on the site `(C, J)` for all `n : ℕ`. These abelian
22	groups are defined as the `Ext`-groups from the constant abelian
23	sheaf with values `ℤ` (actually `ULift ℤ`) to `F`.
24	
25	We also define `Sheaf.cohomologyPresheaf F n : Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat`
26	which is the presheaf which sends `U` to the `n`th `Ext`-group
27	from the free abelian sheaf generated by the presheaf
28	of sets `yoneda.obj U` to `F`.
29	
30	## TODO
31	* if `U` is a terminal object of `C`, define an isomorphism
32	  `(F.cohomologyPresheaf n).obj (Opposite.op U) ≃+ Sheaf.H F n`.
33	* if `U : C`, define an isomorphism
34	  `(F.cohomologyPresheaf n).obj (Opposite.op U) ≃+ Sheaf.H (F.over U) n`.
35	
36	-/
37	
38	@[expose] public noncomputable section
39	
40	assert_not_exists TwoSidedIdeal
41	
42	universe w' w v u
43	
44	namespace CategoryTheory
45	
46	open Abelian
47	
48	variable {C : Type u} [Category.{v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
49	
50	namespace Sheaf
51	
52	section
53	
54	variable (F : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{w})
55	  [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{w}] [HasExt.{w'} (Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{w})]
56	
57	/-- The cohomology of an abelian sheaf in degree `n`. -/
58	def H (n : ℕ) : Type w' :=
59	  Ext ((constantSheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{w}).obj (AddCommGrpCat.of (ULift ℤ))) F n
60	deriving AddCommGroup
61	
62	variable (J) in
63	/-- Cohomology of an abelian sheaf in degree `n` as a functor. -/
64	def cohomologyFunctor (n : ℕ) :
65	    Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{w} ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{w'} :=
66	  (extFunctor.{w'} n).obj <|
67	    .op <| ((constantSheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{w}).obj (AddCommGrpCat.of (ULift ℤ)))
68	deriving Functor.Additive
69	
70	lemma cohomologyFunctor_obj (n : ℕ) (F : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{w}) :
71	    (cohomologyFunctor J n).obj F = F.H n :=
72	  rfl
73	
74	lemma subsingleton_H_of_isZero {F : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{w}} (h : Limits.IsZero F) (n : ℕ) :
75	    Subsingleton (F.H n) := by
76	  rw [← cohomologyFunctor_obj]
77	  apply AddCommGrpCat.subsingleton_of_isZero
78	  exact Functor.map_isZero (cohomologyFunctor J n) h
79	
80	end
81	
82	section
83	
84	variable [HasSheafify J AddCommGrpCat.{v}] [HasExt.{w'} (Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v})]
85	
86	variable (J) in
87	/-- The bifunctor which sends an abelian sheaf `F` and an object `U` to the
88	`n`th Ext-group from the free abelian sheaf generated by the
89	presheaf of sets `yoneda.obj U` to `F`. -/
90	def cohomologyPresheafFunctor (n : ℕ) :
91	    Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v} ⥤ Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{w'} :=
92	  Functor.flip
93	    (Functor.op (yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj
94	      AddCommGrpCat.free ⋙ presheafToSheaf _ _) ⋙ extFunctor n)
95	
96	/-- Given an abelian sheaf `F`, this is the presheaf which sends `U`
97	to the `n`th Ext-group from the free abelian sheaf generated by the
98	presheaf of sets `yoneda.obj U` to `F`. -/
99	abbrev cohomologyPresheaf (F : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v}) (n : ℕ) :
100	    Cᵒᵖ ⥤ AddCommGrpCat.{w'} :=
101	  (cohomologyPresheafFunctor J n).obj F
102	
103	/-- Given an abelian sheaf `F` on `(C, J)`, `n : ℕ` and `X : C`, this is
104	the degree-`n` sheaf cohomology of `X` with values in `F`. -/
105	abbrev H' (F : Sheaf J AddCommGrpCat.{v}) (n : ℕ) (X : C) :
106	    AddCommGrpCat.{w'} :=
107	  (F.cohomologyPresheaf n).obj (Opposite.op X)
108	
109	end
110	
111	end Sheaf
112	
113	end CategoryTheory

import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK
open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry CategoryTheory.Abelian

universe u v

namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

-- Probe: HModule' as scaffolded inside namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
noncomputable abbrev HModule' (k : Type u) [Field k]
    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
    [CategoryTheory.HasExt.{u} (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (n : ℕ) (X : C) : Type u :=
  Ext ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
    ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X)) F n

end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme

noncomputable example {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))

  Module.finrank k (Scheme.HModule' k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1 X)

input.new_string:
- Do NOT close with vacuous types (`PUnit`, `ULift PUnit`, `ModuleCat.of k PUnit`, etc.) — `cechCochain_OC` and `cechCohomology_OC` must be the genuine objects.
- Do NOT change the iter-011 closure of `Genus.lean` or the iter-009/010 declarations of `HModule` / `HModule_zero_linearEquiv`.

### Iter-013 plan: `HModule'` (cohomology of an open in `ModuleCat k` flavor) — Phase A step 6 *proper* sub-step (Mayer-Vietoris prerequisite)

**Status: PLAN-AGENT FIRST PASS COMPLETE; REFACTOR ROUND PENDING** (iter-013, this iteration). With iter-012's Čech-side carriers in scope, iter-013 opens the next Path-2 Phase A step 6 *proper* sub-step: the `ModuleCat k`-flavored *cohomology of an open* — a parallel to Mathlib's `Sheaf.H' F n X : AddCommGrpCat.{w'}` (file `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/Basic.lean`, L105) for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)`. This is the API entry point that downstream Mayer-Vietoris LES arguments (queued iter-014+) and Čech-comparison theorems (queued iter-015+) will both consume.

**Why this is the iter-013 target.** The natural follow-on from iter-012's `cechCohomology_OC` is to attack **either** the comparison theorem `cechCohomology_OC C 𝒰 n ≅ HModule k (toModuleKSheaf C) n` (multi-iteration; the technical heart of Path-2) **or** the Mayer-Vietoris LES that lets us decompose `HModule k (toModuleKSheaf C) n` over a 2-affine cover (also multi-iteration; cleaner decomposition). Both attacks need cohomology-of-an-open as a prerequisite — Mathlib's Mayer-Vietoris LES (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/SheafCohomology/MayerVietoris.lean`) is stated on `F.H' n X` (the `Sheaf.H'` form), and a `ModuleCat k`-LES will need the corresponding `HModule'`. iter-013 lays this prerequisite; iter-014+ uses it to state and prove a `ModuleCat k`-LES; iter-015+ then specialises to a 2-affine cover of a proper $k$-curve.

Iter-013 is **not** the comparison theorem and **not** the LES — both are multi-iteration. Iter-013 is the smallest standalone unit that has standalone value (every downstream Mayer-Vietoris / comparison step consumes the `HModule'` carrier).

**Mathematical content.** For a Grothendieck site `(C, J)`, a sheaf `F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)`, an integer `n`, and an object `X : C`:
\[ H^n_{\ModuleCat\,k}(F, X) := \mathrm{Ext}^n\bigl((\mathtt{presheafToSheaf}\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k)).\mathrm{obj}((\yoneda \circ \free_k).\mathrm{obj}\,X),\; F\bigr) \]
where `free_k : Type u ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} k` is `ModuleCat.free k`. This mirrors Mathlib's `Sheaf.H' F n X = Ext ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj (yoneda ⋙ AddCommGrpCat.free).obj X) F n` line-by-line, with `AddCommGrpCat.free → ModuleCat.free k` and codomain `Type u` (not `ModuleCat.{u} k`, since `Abelian.Ext` returns a bare `Type` carrying `Module k` via `Abelian.Ext.instModule` through the `Linear k` enrichment).

**Probe (iter-013 plan-agent, this pass).** Two `lean_run_code` probes against the post-iter-012 project state:

```lean
import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK
open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry CategoryTheory.Abelian

universe u v

noncomputable abbrev HModule' (k : Type u) [Field k]
    {C : Type v} [Category.{u, v} C] {J : GrothendieckTopology C}
    [HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)] [HasSheafify J (ModuleCat.{u} k)]
    [CategoryTheory.HasExt.{u} (Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k))]
    (F : Sheaf J (ModuleCat.{u} k)) (n : ℕ) (X : C) : Type u :=
  Ext ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
    ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X)) F n
-- {success: true, diagnostics: []}

-- Smoke test: Module.finrank synthesis through abbrev unfolding
noncomputable example {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    (X : Opens C.left.toTopCat) : ℕ :=
  Module.finrank k (Scheme.HModule' k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1 X)
-- {success: true, diagnostics: []}
```

The `noncomputable abbrev` form (rather than `def`) is required for the same reason as iter-009's `HModule`: instance synthesis must see through the wrapper to find `Abelian.Ext.instModule` and `Abelian.Ext.instAddCommGroup`; under `def`, `Module.finrank k (HModule' k F n X)` would fail to typecheck. The `[HasWeakSheafify J (Type u)]` typeclass is required (and auto-inferable for `Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat`, probe-verified).

**Iter-013 work:**

1. **Refactor agent.** Append a single new declaration to `AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK.lean` (no new file; the existing file already imports the toolchain and is the natural home given the iter-009/010 `HModule` family lives there):
   - `Scheme.HModule' k F n X : Type u` — body `:= sorry`. The `ModuleCat k`-flavored cohomology of `X` with values in `F`. Placement: inside `namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme` (parallel to iter-009 `HModule` and iter-010 `HModule_zero_linearEquiv`), inserted between the existing `HModule_zero_linearEquiv` (L203–L210) and the closing `end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme` (L212). ~10 LOC including signature + docstring.
2. **Prover agent.** Close the body with the probe-confirmed one-liner:
   ```lean
   Ext ((presheafToSheaf _ _).obj
     ((yoneda ⋙ (Functor.whiskeringRight _ _ _).obj (ModuleCat.free k)).obj X)) F n
   ```

Pre-prover: 10 sorries (post-refactor: 9 + 1 new). Post-prover: 9 sorries (back to baseline: 8 protected + 1 deferred `representable`).

**What iter-013 does and does not.** Iter-013 *defines* `HModule'` — a `ModuleCat k`-flavored cohomology-of-an-open API parallel to Mathlib's `Sheaf.H'`. Iter-013 does **not** prove the `H' = H` iso for `X` terminal (Mathlib's open TODO, comment in `Basic.lean` line 32), define a `cohomologyPresheaf` functor (queued iter-014+), or scaffold the Mayer-Vietoris LES (queued iter-014+). The iter-013 scaffold is the smallest standalone unit with standalone value; every downstream Mayer-Vietoris step consumes `HModule'`.

321	  \]
322	\end{definition}
323	
324	\begin{proof}
325	  \leanok
326	  \uses{def:Scheme_cechCochain_OC}
327	  Direct application of \lstinline{HomologicalComplex.homology} on the cochain complex of Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_cechCochain_OC}. The closure body is the one-line term \lstinline{(Scheme.cechCochain_OC C 𝒰).homology n}.
328	\end{proof}
329	
330	\begin{remark}[What iter-012 does and does not do]
331	  Iter-012 \emph{defines} the Čech cochain complex and Čech cohomology object of the structure sheaf for an arbitrary indexed open cover. Iter-012 does \emph{not} prove that this Čech cohomology agrees with the derived-functor cohomology $\HModule\,k\,(\toModuleKSheaf C)\,n$ of Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_HModule} — the comparison theorem requires the cover to be \emph{acyclic} (e.g., all opens affine, plus Serre's vanishing on affine schemes for quasi-coherent sheaves), neither of which is iter-012 work. The comparison theorem is queued for iter-013+; the affine-finiteness step (each cochain group is finite-dimensional over $k$ when the cover is affine and the curve is proper) is queued for iter-014+; the dimension-vanishing step (degree $> 1$ vanishes on a curve) is queued for iter-015+.
332	\end{remark}
333	
334	\begin{remark}[Why two declarations and not one]
335	  Splitting $\check C^\bullet$ from $\check H^n$ matches Mathlib's standard pattern (\lstinline{cechComplexFunctor} returns a complex; \lstinline{HomologicalComplex.homology} extracts a degree). The complex is useful in its own right (e.g., for explicit cocycle / coboundary calculations, or for showing exactness on a specific cover), so we expose it as a separate API surface.
336	\end{remark}
337	
338	\section{Use in the project}
339	
340	The composition of the four typeclass instances of Sections~\ref{sec:moduleK_prereqs}–\ref{sec:moduleK_ext}, Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}, and Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_HModule} delivers Phase~A step~5 in full and bridges to Phase~A step~6:
341	
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item For $C : \Over\,(\Spec\,(\CommRingCat\,k))$, the $\ModuleCat\,k$-flavoured cohomology
344	    \[
345	      H^n_{\Module\,k}(C, \struct C) \;:=\; \mathtt{HModule}\,k\,(\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,n
346	    \]
347	    is well-defined and automatically carries a $\Module\,k$ instance, so $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,(\HModule\,k\,(\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1) : \mathbb N$ is well-defined.
348	  \item The forgetful functor $\ModuleCat\,k \to \AddCommGrpCat$ recovers the iter-004 abelian-group cohomology (Definition~\ref{def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf_forgetCompare}), so the new $\HModule$ commutes with the existing $\Sheaf.\mathtt H$ on the underlying type level.
349	  \item Phase~A step~6 (Serre finiteness, $\finrank_k H^i(C, \mathcal F) < \infty$) remains the deep work to come, but is now expressible inside Mathlib's standard $\Module\,k$ API: the statement of Serre finiteness becomes a $\Module.\mathtt{Finite}\,k\,(\HModule\,k\,F\,n)$ predicate.
350	\end{itemize}
351	
352	\section{Mathlib gap}
353	
354	After Section~\ref{sec:scheme_toModuleKSheaf} is implemented, this chapter has no remaining Mathlib gap. The construction reuses only the following established Mathlib pieces:

358	  \item \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.\Gamma SpecIso}, \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.app}, \lstinline{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.presheaf} (existing Mathlib API for the structure morphism and the structure-sheaf restriction maps).
359	  \item \lstinline{Algebra.compHom} / \lstinline{RingHom.toAlgebra} (algebra-from-ring-map).
360	  \item \lstinline{CategoryTheory.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_forget} (concrete-category sheaf criterion; transfers the sheaf condition between $\CommRingCat$ and $\ModuleCat\,k$).
361	\end{itemize}
362	
363	Nothing genuinely new is required from Mathlib. The iter-006 plan-agent \texttt{lean\_run\_code} probe verifies the full $\sim$80-LOC construction end-to-end against Mathlib \texttt{b80f227}.

# Refactor Directive — iter-013

**Iteration:** 013
**Stage:** prover (this directive scopes the iter-013 refactor; the iter-013 prover round follows after the refactor)
**Strategic context:** Phase A step 6 *proper* sub-step — Mayer-Vietoris prerequisite. With iter-012's Čech-side carriers (`Scheme.cechCochain_OC`, `Scheme.cechCohomology_OC`) in scope and verified clean (kernel-only axioms; sorry count back to baseline 9), the next Path-2 sub-step is the `ModuleCat k`-flavored cohomology of an open. This is the API entry point that downstream Mayer-Vietoris LES (queued iter-014+) and Čech-vs-derived-functor comparison theorems (queued iter-015+) both consume.

## Problem statement

Mathlib's sheaf cohomology API has two flavors:

- `Sheaf.H F n : Type w'` — cohomology in degree `n` of an abelian sheaf `F` (the "global" cohomology). The iter-009 declaration `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule k F n` is the `ModuleCat k`-flavored mirror.

# Project Progress

## Current Stage
